perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Information will be used solely for giving effect to the Business Under Discussion and further that it will not disclose to any person (i) the Information and (ii) the fact that the Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or that the Receiving Party have inspected any portion of the Information, for an y reason or purpose whatsoever, unless the Providing Party has consented in writing to such disclosure or except as stated otherwise herein or as required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Information should ideally be used for the Business Under Discussion and will endeavor not to disclose to any person (i) the Information and (ii) the fact that the Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or that the Receiving Party have inspected any portion of the Information, for any reason or purpose, unless the Providing Party has consented or unless disclosure is somehow required.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be used solely' to 'should ideally be used' and 'endeavor not to disclose' introduces ambiguity. Also, replacing the detailed legal exceptions with 'unless disclosure is somehow required' is vague.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(f) of the GDPR requires personal data to be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorized or unlawful processing. The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the 'endeavor not to disclose', creating doubt about the level of protection applied to the data. It also introduces uncertainty about what constitutes a 'required' disclosure, potentially leading to data breaches, which contradicts the principle of integrity and confidentiality outlined in the GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’).
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect personal data, potentially contradicting GDPR Article 5(1)(f). The original text states the Information 'will be used solely' for a specific purpose, whereas the changed text uses 'should ideally be used', which weakens the obligation. Further, the original text says the Receiving Party 'will not disclose', but the changed text says the Receiving Party 'will endeavor not to disclose', introducing a lack of commitment. Also, 'unless the Providing Party has consented in writing to such disclosure or except as stated otherwise herein or as required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request' is replaced by 'unless the Providing Party has consented or unless disclosure is somehow required', which is vague and undermines the original obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that personal data should be 'processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorized or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage'. The changes weaken the security and increase the risk of unlawful processing. This aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, as the ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce the obligation and can result in non-compliance with regulatory requirements.

perturbation.original_text: In th e event that the Receiving Party is required by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Receiving Party agrees that it will provide the Providing Party with prompt written notice of such request(s) prior to the required disclosure and the Providing Party shall use reasonable efforts, at its own expense, to seek a protective o rder or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party may be required by some law to disclose any of the Information, the Receiving Party agrees that it will attempt to notify the Providing Party with some notice of such request prior to disclosure; the Providing Party may seek a protective order or other remedy.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'is required by applicable law or regulation or by legal process' with 'may be required by some law' introduces ambiguity. Replacing 'prompt written notice' with 'attempt to notify with some notice' and 'shall use reasonable efforts' with 'may seek' also weakens the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Data Protection Act 2018 (UK) - Schedule 2, Part 1, Paragraph 4
perturbation.law_citation: Schedule 2, Part 1, Paragraph 4, Data Protection Act 2018 (UK)
perturbation.law_explanation: Schedule 2, Part 1, Paragraph 4 of the Data Protection Act 2018 (UK) outlines conditions for processing for law enforcement purposes, requiring that processing be necessary and proportionate. The modified text's ambiguity about 'some law' and the weakened notification obligation ('attempt to notify with some notice') undermine the necessity and proportionality assessment, potentially leading to unlawful disclosure, which contradicts the Act.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'prompt written notice' and 'reasonable efforts' to seek remedies, while the modified contract weakens these obligations to 'attempt to notify with some notice' and 'may seek' a remedy. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that the change introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligations. The Law Explanation suggests this ambiguity undermines necessity and proportionality assessment, potentially leading to unlawful disclosure, conflicting with the Data Protection Act 2018 (UK). While no law snippets were provided, the weakened language from 'is required' to 'may be required' substantially lowers the bar for compliance, creating ambiguity in the legal obligation, thus leading to a contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: If any of the Parties determine that they do not wish to procee d or continue with the Business Under Discussion , it will promptly advise the other Party of that decision in writing or by email. Upon receipt of such communication the Receiving Party shall destroy promptly all documents furnished and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in who le or in part of such Information.
perturbation.changed_text: If any of the Parties determine that they do not wish to proceed or continue with the Business Under Discussion, they should advise the other Party of that decision. Upon receipt of such communication, the Receiving Party will likely destroy most documents furnished and try not to retain copies of such Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'it will promptly advise' with 'they should advise' weakens the obligation to notify. Replacing 'shall destroy promptly all documents' with 'will likely destroy most documents' and 'will not retain any copies' with 'try not to retain copies' introduces significant ambiguity and weakens data destruction requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: GDPR - Article 17 (Right to Erasure / 'Right to be Forgotten')
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the GDPR grants individuals the right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'), requiring data controllers to erase personal data without undue delay under certain conditions. By changing 'shall destroy promptly all documents' to 'will likely destroy most documents' and 'will not retain any copies' to 'try not to retain copies,' the modified text weakens the obligation to delete data, potentially violating the right to erasure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text weakens the data destruction requirements, creating a contradiction with GDPR Article 17. The original text states the Receiving Party 'shall destroy promptly all documents' and 'will not retain any copies,' indicating a clear and mandatory obligation. The changed text, however, uses 'will likely destroy most documents' and 'try not to retain copies,' introducing ambiguity and discretion, which undermines the 'right to be forgotten' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'the data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay'. The modification fails to ensure complete and timely data erasure as required by GDPR.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of loss or dama ge to the Providing Party as a result of breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall be liable only in respect of loss or damage resulting directly from the breach, and no liability will be accepted in respect of any special, indi rect, incidental or consequential losses or damages whatsoever (including, but not limited to, lost profits or revenue) suffered by the Providing Party or any person connected with the Providing Party, arising out of the performance or breach of this Agree ment, whether claims for said losses or damages are premised on contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of loss or damage to the Providing Party as a result of breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may be liable in respect of loss or damage resulting directly from the breach, and no liability will be accepted in respect of typical indirect losses or damages suffered by the Providing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be liable' to 'may be liable' introduces ambiguity. The phrasing 'typical indirect losses or damages' is also very vague and likely unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3 of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 applies to business liability for negligence, restricting the extent to which liability can be excluded or limited. The modified clause, by using 'may be liable' and excluding 'typical indirect losses,' attempts to limit liability for breach of contract in a vague and ambiguous way. This could be deemed unreasonable under the Act, particularly if the exclusion is interpreted very broadly or is not brought to the other party's attention.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be liable' to 'may be liable' introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's responsibility, weakening the legal obligation. Furthermore, the phrase 'typical indirect losses or damages' is vague and unenforceable, which attempts to limit liability for breach of contract in a vague and ambiguous way. According to the Law Explanation, this could be deemed unreasonable under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, particularly if the exclusion is interpreted very broadly. This introduces Ambiguity as the 'may be liable' phrasing creates doubt about the Receiving Party's commitment, while the 'typical indirect losses' language lacks the specificity required for legal enforcement, potentially leading to disputes about the scope of liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977. This alteration introduces an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, making it difficult to enforce the contract and potentially leading to non-compliance, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in acc ordance with the laws of England and Wales without giving effect to conflict of law principles, and the Parties agree to submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the English courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be somehow governed, and the Parties agree to submit to some jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Completely removes the governing law and jurisdiction specifics, making enforcement almost impossible. The vague 'somehow governed' and 'some jurisdiction' make the agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 25 of the Brussels I Regulation (Recast) specifies the requirements for jurisdiction agreements. By removing the specific designation of English courts and the governing law of England and Wales, the modified text fails to meet the requirements of this Article. This lack of clarity about jurisdiction and applicable law undermines the predictability and enforceability of the agreement, conflicting with the purpose of Article 25, which is to ensure legal certainty regarding the resolution of disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ts proceedings as soon as the designated court has been seised and until such time as the latter court declares that it has no jurisdiction under the exclusive choice-of-court agreement. This is to ensure that, in such a situation, the designated court has priority to decide on the validity of the agreement and on the extent to which the agreement applies to the dispute pending before it. The designated court should be able to proceed irrespective of whether the non-designated court has already decided on the stay of proceedings. This exception should not cover situations where the parties have entered into conflicting exclusive choice-of-court agreements or where a court designated in an exclusive choice-of-court agreement has been seised first. In such cases, the general lis pendens rule of this Regulation should apply. (23) This Regulation should provide for a flexible mechanism allowing the courts of the Member States to take into account proceedings pending before the courts of third States, considering in particular whether a judgment of a third State will be capable of recognition and enforcement in the Member State concerned under the law of that Member State and the proper administration of justice. (24) When taking into account the proper administration of justice, the court of the Member State concerned should assess all the circumstances of the case before it. Such circumstances may include connections between the facts of the case and the parties and the third State concerned, the stage to which the proceedings in the third State have progressed by the time proceedings are initiated in the court of the Member State and whether or not the court of the third State can be expected to give a judgment within a reasonable time. That assessment may also include consideration of the question whether the court of the third State has exclusive jurisdiction in the particular case in circumstances where a court of a Member State would have exclusive jurisdiction. (25) The notion of provisional, including protective, measures should include, for example, protective orders aimed at obtaining information or preserving evidence as referred to in Articles 6 and 7 of Directive 2004/48/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 29 April 2004 on the enforcement of intellectual property rights ( 11 ) . It should not include measures which are not of a protective nature, such as measures ordering the hearing of a witness. This should be without prejudice to the application of Council Regulation (EC) No 1206/2001 of 28 May 2001 on cooperation between the courts of the Member States in the taking of evidence in civil or commercial matters ( 12 ) . (26) Mutual trust in the administration of justice in the Union justifies the principle that judgments given in a Member State should be recognised in all Member States without the need for any special procedure. In addition, the aim of making cross-border litigation less time-consuming and costly justifies the abolition of the declaration of enforceability prior to enforcement in the Member State addressed. As a result, a judgment given by the courts of a Member State should be treated as if it had been given in the Member State addressed. (27) For the purposes of the free circulation of judgments, a judgment given in a Member State should be recognised and enforced in another Member State even if it is given against a person not domiciled in a Member State. (28) Where a judgment contains a measure or order which is not known in the law of the Member State addressed, that measure or order, including any right indicated therein, should, to the extent possible, be adapted to one which, under the law of that Member State, has equivalent effects attached to it and pursues similar aims. How, and by whom, the adaptation is to be carried out should be determined by each Member State. (29) The direct enforcement in the Member State addressed of a judgment given in another Member State without a
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific governing law and jurisdiction, replacing them with vague references ('somehow governed' and 'some jurisdiction'). This directly undermines the enforceability of the agreement. The Law Explanation states that this change fails to meet the requirements of Article 25 of the Brussels I Regulation (Recast), compromising legal certainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses the importance of designated courts and their jurisdiction, highlighting the issues created when agreements lack clarity on these points.
